articles.title: Brakes on heatwave for a while; cloudy Sunday, predicts IMD - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Lucknow: Relief from scorching heat continued in the city for second day on Saturday. Similar conditions will continue for next two days, followed by gradual rise in mercury levels again.
Temperatures that had fallen below normal following thunderstorm in the wee-hours of Friday, continued to remain low on Saturday. Though no thunderstorm was witnessed on Saturday, strong moist winds kept weather conditions comfortable.
Maximum temperature was 34 degrees Celsius, 6.1 units below normal, while minimum temperature was 24.5 degrees Celsius, a degree below normal. Relative humidity was high and oscillated between 47%-79%.
India Meteorological Department (IMD), Lucknow, predicted maximum and minimum temperatures on Sunday would be around 33 and 24 degrees, Celsius respectively. Morning will be partly cloudy and cloud cover will increase during the day.
It also forecast one or two spells of thundershowers in the afternoon or evening accompanied by lightning along with winds measuring 40-50 kmph, gusting up to 60 kmph.
Meanwhile, most parts of the state too experienced drop in mercury levels by 4 to 7 degrees Celsius on Saturday.
Some parts were lashed by thunder/dust storms.
Moradabad was hottest place in the state maximum temperature of 38.5 degrees Celsius, followed by Fatehgarh at 38 and Lakhimpur Kheri at 37. Etawah and Gorakhpur recorded lowest maximum temperatures at 32.6 degrees Celsius each, while Barabanki recorded 33.5 degrees Celsius.
“Thunderstorms accompanied by winds 80-90 kmph are expected to lash several places in the state on Sunday. The wind gust at isolated areas may reach up to 100 kmph,” said Mohammad Danish, senior scientist at IMD Lucknow.
“Change in weather condition is being driven by a western disturbance and moisture-laden easterly winds from the Bay of Bengal. Similar weather conditions are expected across parts of UP for the next few days”, he explained.
Meteorologists said the current weather system has temporarily ended heatwave in the state, but temperatures will start rising gradually at the beginning of Jun and increase by 5 to 7 degrees Celsius in the first week, thereby reviving the intense hot conditions.

articles.title: PM Modi advises hydration and enjoying desi drinks amid heatwave - The News Mill
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Prime Minister Narendra Modi on Sunday urged citizens to stay hydrated and take precautions as summer temperatures rise across India, recommending the enjoyment of traditional ‘desi drinks’ to cope with the heat.
Speaking during his Mann Ki Baat programme, PM Modi highlighted the widespread popularity of mangoes, noting that “there’s hardly a home in India where mangoes are not talked about in summer.”
He said, “It is very hot in most parts of the country right now. Strong sun, hot winds, it is very important to take care of yourself in such weather. Keep drinking water. If at all you have to go out in the sun, do so with caution. Don’t forget the guidelines issued by various government departments in this regard.”
PM Modi added that ways to combat the heat are often found in traditional Indian kitchens, mentioning aap panna, lassi, and buttermilk.
“You must have noticed that as the heat increases, the taste of home cooking changes, the type of cooking changes. At some places, water from earthen pots is brought forth; in others, curd starts setting, and in others, raw mangoes start boiling – and then starts the time of Indigenous drinks,” he said.
He continued, “You are also familiar with these Desi drinks. If you go to North India, you will find aam Panna in many places, the taste of raw mango, and relief from the heat. If you go to Punjab or Haryana, you’ll find lassi in large glasses. Buttermilk in Rajasthan and Gujarat is like a companion to every meal.”
The Prime Minister emphasised that these drinks represent timeless traditions and the spirit of ‘Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat’.
“Sattu sherbet in Bihar, Jharkhand, and Eastern Uttar Pradesh is simply amazing – it fills the stomach and provides strength. Kokum sherbet and Sol Kadhi in Konkan and Goa. Panakam, Neer Mor, Sambaram in South India, and Bael Pana in Odisha aren’t just drinks, they’re a part of the traditions of different regions of India. And they also reflect the spirit of ‘Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat.’,” he said.
He also noted, “And one thing you must remember: most of these things come from our own kitchens, from our farms and fields. There’s no big branding, but they embody the experience of generations. You too enjoy these desi drinks during the summer.”
PM Modi further discussed the various mango varieties found throughout the country, stating each region has its own distinctive mango and flavour.
“As soon as summer arrives, another topic of discussion starts in every household: and that is mangoes. Mangoes are a common topic of discussion. There’s hardly a home in India where mangoes are not talked about in Summer. Every region… its own mango, its own flavor, its own aroma. Maharashtra and Konkan’s Hapus, Alphonso, Gujarat’s Kesar, it’s the soul of Aamras, Uttar Pradesh’s Dussehri, and my Kashi’s Langra,” he said.
He added, “Incidentally, the Langra mango has a special quality: it often remains green even after ripening. Bihar’s Zardalu, whose aroma can be recognized from afar. Chausa, Malda – each name has memories associated with it. Go to South India, and there’s Banganapalli, Totapuri, Neelam, Malgova, Bengal’s Himsagar, Odisha, and Andhra Pradesh’s Suvarnarekha. In other words, as the place changes, the mango’s appearance, color, and taste also change.”
The Prime Minister praised farmers involved in mango cultivation, stating, “Today, through ‘Mann Ki Baat’, I will praise my farmer brothers and sisters involved in mango cultivation. You are not just ordinary farmers… you are very special for the agricultural economy of the country. May you keep shining like this.”

articles.title: 2 Army trainees die of heat stroke, complications - SunStar Publishing Inc.
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
TWO aspiring soldiers died during a military training in Capas, Tarlac over severe health complications, the Philippine Army Training Command (Tracom) confirmed Saturday, May 30, 2026.
In a statement, the Tracom said Candidate Soldiers (CS) Johndyl Lemosniro of New Corella, Davao del Norte, and Junel Flores of Lamitan City, Basilan suffered severe health complications brought on by extreme physical exertion in high temperatures during the training.
Upon showing symptoms, the two trainees were immediately brought to the O'Donnell Medical Treatment Facility (CODMTF).
“Because they were unconscious and in critical condition, medical staff immediately performed endotracheal intubation to secure their airways and assist their breathing,” the statement read.
“To ensure they received the highest level of specialized care, they were promptly transferred to the Tarlac Provincial Hospital for advanced laboratory testing and intensive treatment. Regrettably, despite the exhaustive life-saving efforts of the medical teams at both facilities, both trainees passed away,” it added.
Both trainees, according to the attending physicians, succumbed to brain complications and swelling (encephalopathy), which was a direct result of the severe heat stroke.
The Philippine Army expressed sympathy to the bereaved families.
It also assured its steadfast commitment to the welfare, health, and safety of all trainees undergoing military instruction.
“We assure the public and the families of our recruits that stringent safety policies, medical protocols, and precautionary procedures remain enforced,” it said. (TPM/SunStar Philippines)
